summary: Big moves on the SE BeltLine today with Reynoldstown finally seeing construction restart and Inman Park welcoming a fresh Asian comfort food spot. Meanwhile, the airport chaos continues with ICE agents wandering around Hartsfield-Jackson while TSA workers struggle without pay.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown Corner Near BeltLine Finally Sees Construction Start
topStory.summary: After sitting cleared for nearly five years, that corner lot where Chester Avenue meets Fulton Terrace is finally getting some action. Embry Development took over the stalled project from Toll Brothers and is now building 85 townhomes and condos just a block from the Eastside Trail. The site's been a neighborhood eyesore since 2021, but locals should start seeing real progress on what's being called Camber Crossing.
topStory.body:
You know that empty corner where Chester Avenue hits Fulton Terrace in Reynoldstown? The one that's been sitting there like a missing tooth since they cleared it back in 2021? Well, finally, there's some movement. Embry Development has taken over what was originally a Toll Brothers project and is actually putting shovels in the ground for 85 residential units.

The project, now called Camber Crossing, will mix townhomes with for-sale condos about a block west of the BeltLine's Eastside Trail. It's prime real estate in a neighborhood that's seen plenty of changes, and honestly, anything is better than that vacant lot that's been staring at neighbors for years. Embry bought the 2-acre site from Toll Brothers two years ago and kept the original permits, which probably saved everyone a lot of headaches.

What makes this interesting is the timing. Reynoldstown has been in this sweet spot where it's close enough to the BeltLine action but hasn't completely priced out longtime residents yet. This kind of development usually signals that the gentrification wave is picking up speed, so we'll see how the neighborhood handles 85 new units of what's likely to be pretty pricey housing.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction site at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace in Reynoldstown showing early groundwork for the Camber Crossing development

stories.headline: Decatur's New Village Tackles the Missing Middle Housing Crisis
stories.summary: Cottages at Midway is launching next week as Decatur's answer to the affordable housing crunch. This 18-house development on Midway Road offers standalone homes around shared courtyards, targeting the gap between apartments and $700K+ single-family homes. It's mixed-income housing designed to keep some affordability in a city where median home values have gone through the roof.
stories.body:
With median home values in Decatur pushing past $700K, finding anything remotely affordable ITP has become a real challenge. That's where Cottages at Midway comes in, a 2.1-acre development on Midway Road that's trying to fill that missing middle gap with 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards and greenspace.

The concept is pretty smart: take those underused lots and create a village-style community that feels more like a neighborhood than a typical subdivision. The homes are designed to be more accessible than Decatur's typical single-family offerings, though 'affordable' is relative when you're talking about anything near downtown Decatur these days. Still, it's a step toward addressing the reality that teachers, firefighters, and other working folks are getting priced out.

What's interesting about this project is the mixed-income component and the focus on community design. The shared courtyards and greenspace are meant to encourage neighbors to actually know each other, which is becoming rarer in new developments. The soft launch starts next week, so we'll see if there's appetite for this kind of intentional community living in Decatur's hot real estate market.
stories.imageAlt: Site map showing layout of Cottages at Midway development with 18 homes arranged around courtyards

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Soft Shell Crab Bao to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez just opened their first restaurant on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail. Mushi Ni serves Asian-American comfort food with everything from Peking chicken platters to crab Rangoon puffs and Thai tea martinis. It's the couple's first brick-and-mortar after years of pop-ups around town.
stories.body:
After years of doing pop-ups around Atlanta, chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez finally have their own place. Mushi Ni opened today on Elizabeth Street in Inman Park, just a hop from the Freedom Park Trail, serving up Asian-American comfort food that reflects both chefs' backgrounds and their time in some pretty serious kitchens.

Tanya worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York before moving to Atlanta, and you can see that level of execution in dishes like their soft shell crab bao and chicken longanisa. The menu hits that sweet spot between approachable and interesting, with Peking chicken platters that feed two for $25 and cocktails like Thai tea martinis that actually make sense. The umami fries and crab Rangoon puffs show they're not taking themselves too seriously.

The location is perfect for Inman Park, tucked away enough to feel like a neighborhood spot but accessible from the Freedom Park Trail for cyclists and runners. It's exactly the kind of chef-driven, casual place that works in this part of town, where people want good food without the pretension. Plus, having lunch and dinner service means you can grab bao on your lunch break or settle in for cocktails after work.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken platter at Mushi Ni restaurant

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue's Austin-Style Brisket Hits the BeltLine
stories.summary: John Lewis brought his Austin barbecue game to Ansley Mall, complete with a spectacular upstairs smokehouse overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. Six 22-foot smokers run 24/7 in what looks like the fanciest engine room in Georgia, turning out some of the best brisket you'll find in Atlanta. The pitmaster helped weld those smokers himself from 1,000-gallon propane tanks.
stories.body:
The smokehouse at Lewis Barbecue is unlike anything you've seen in Atlanta. Most barbecue joints hide their smokers in back rooms or outdoor sheds, but John Lewis put his upstairs in a spacious loft with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. Six massive 22-foot smokers fill the space, running 24 hours a day in staggered eight-hour shifts like some kind of beautiful, smoky factory.

Lewis, the pitmaster behind this operation, actually helped weld those smokers out of 1,000-gallon propane tanks. The guy knows his way around both metal and meat, having honed his craft in Austin before bringing that Texas-style approach to Atlanta. The result is some genuinely outstanding brisket that holds its own against anything you'll find in the city, with that perfect smoke ring and tender texture that takes serious skill to achieve.

The location at Ansley Mall puts it right in the heart of BeltLine foot traffic, which is smart positioning for a barbecue joint that can handle volume. The pedestrian bridge connection makes it an easy stop for anyone walking or biking the trail, and that upstairs smokehouse becomes part of the entertainment. You can actually watch the process while you eat, which beats staring at your phone.
stories.imageAlt: Loaded barbecue platter with brisket and sides at Lewis Barbecue

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's New Food Hall Opens in East Atlanta
stories.summary: The guy who created NYC's Gansevoort Market is opening his third Atlanta food concept at Vela Park near East Lake Golf Club. Vela Park Food Works launches in mid-April with six chef-driven concepts, craft cocktails, and outdoor dining. It's the centralized retail piece of the 12-acre rental community that's been taking shape on the eastside.
stories.body:
Robert Montwaid has been quietly building a food hall empire in Atlanta, and his latest project might be the most ambitious yet. Vela Park Food Works is set to open in mid-April as the dining centerpiece of the new Vela Park community near East Lake Golf Club, bringing six different chef-driven concepts under one roof with craft cocktails and plenty of outdoor dining space.

Montwaid's track record speaks for itself. He's the founder of New York's Gansevoort Market, and his previous Atlanta ventures have shown he knows how to curate food concepts that work together without stepping on each other's toes. The location at Vela Park puts this squarely in the eastside's ongoing development boom, serving both the new rental community and the broader neighborhood that's been seeing more and more food options.

What's smart about this approach is the integration with the larger Vela Park development. Rather than dropping a food hall into an existing neighborhood, this is designed from the ground up as part of a 12-acre community where people live, work, and eat in the same general area. The outdoor dining component makes sense for Atlanta's climate, and having six different concepts means there's something for everyone whether you live there or you're just passing through the eastside.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works showing dining area and food stalls

stories.headline: ICE Agents Wander Atlanta Airport as TSA Crisis Deepens
stories.summary: Federal immigration agents are now roaming Hartsfield-Jackson after Trump deployed them to help with the TSA meltdown, but travelers say they're mostly just standing around. The airport warned of four-hour wait times Monday as 41% of TSA agents called out sick over the weekend. ICE agents aren't doing security screening or immigration enforcement, just providing general security presence.
stories.body:
The scene at Hartsfield-Jackson has become surreal, with armed ICE agents in tactical gear walking around the terminals while massive TSA lines snake through the airport. President Trump deployed Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents to assist as the partial government shutdown enters its sixth week, but their actual role remains unclear to just about everyone, including the travelers watching them stand around.

The numbers tell the story: 41% of Atlanta's TSA workforce called out on Sunday alone, and the airport warned Monday travelers to expect four-hour wait times just to reach security screening. TSA agents have been working without pay since mid-February, and many are either quitting or just not showing up. The ICE agents aren't trained for security screening and they're specifically not doing immigration enforcement, so mostly they're providing what officials call 'general security presence.'

Travelers are frustrated and confused, with many reporting that the ICE agents seem as lost as everyone else. One passenger told reporters the agents are 'not doing anything' while people wait in lines that stretch outside the terminal. Delta even suspended its special services for members of Congress, making lawmakers wait in the same nightmare lines as everyone else. It's a mess that's getting worse by the day, and throwing more federal agents at it doesn't seem to be solving the core problem of TSA workers not getting paid.
stories.imageAlt: ICE agents walking through Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport terminal

stories.headline: Decatur's West Howard Complete Streets Project Nearly Done
stories.summary: After nearly a decade in the works, Decatur's transformation of West Howard Avenue from a car-centric drag strip into a Complete Street is almost finished. The project added two-way cycle tracks with concrete barriers, new sidewalks, and traffic calming from Commerce Drive to the Atlanta city limits. It's designed to make the corridor more people-friendly instead of just a high-speed route for cars.
stories.body:
West Howard Avenue in Decatur used to be one of those roads where cars flew through at highway speeds, making it pretty hostile to anyone on foot or bike. After nearly a decade of planning and construction that started in August, the city's Complete Streets transformation is almost done, and it's a dramatic change from what was there before.

The project stretches about a mile from Commerce Drive near Decatur High School all the way to Paden Circle at the Atlanta city limits, right by East Lake MARTA station. The big addition is a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers that gives cyclists a protected space separated from both cars and pedestrians. New sidewalks, better lighting, and traffic calming measures round out the improvements, all designed to slow down car traffic and make the street work for everyone.

This is exactly the kind of infrastructure investment that cities need to be making if they're serious about reducing car dependency. The timing is perfect with East Lake MARTA station right there, giving people a real alternative to driving everywhere. It's also a good example of how these projects take forever to get done but make a huge difference once they're complete. West Howard was a barrier that cut through neighborhoods, and now it's becoming a connection point.
stories.imageAlt: New protected bike lane and sidewalk on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: Atlanta Council Advances ICE Controls, Fire Union Drama Unfolds
stories.summary: The City Council's Public Safety Committee unanimously advanced resolutions to ban ICE detention facilities in city limits and require APD to document when ICE agents are present during police operations. Meanwhile, dozens of residents showed up to criticize Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the firefighters' first-ever union contract that council already ratified. The full council votes April 20.
stories.body:
Atlanta City Council took on immigration enforcement and labor disputes in the same meeting, with the Public Safety Committee unanimously advancing two resolutions aimed at controlling ICE operations within city limits. The first would ban Immigration and Customs Enforcement detention facilities anywhere in the city, while the second requires Atlanta Police to document whenever ICE agents are present during their operations.

But the bigger drama came from dozens of residents who packed the meeting to blast Mayor Andre Dickens for refusing to sign the city's first-ever contract with the firefighters' union. The council ratified the deal back in April 2025, but Dickens has been sitting on it ever since, leaving firefighters in limbo about their working conditions and pay. Union supporters made it clear they're not happy about the mayor's inaction on what they see as a historic agreement.

The timing is particularly awkward given that ICE agents are currently roaming around Hartsfield-Jackson as part of the TSA crisis response. These resolutions wouldn't affect that federal deployment, but they're clearly Atlanta's way of pushing back against expanded immigration enforcement within city limits. The full council votes on both ICE measures on April 20, which should be interesting given the current political climate around immigration and federal overreach.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council Public Safety Committee meeting with residents in attendance

stories.headline: Decatur Schools Faces Budget Questions Over Controversial Center
stories.summary: City Schools of Decatur is hosting a community meeting Tuesday to answer questions about the upcoming budget and that contentious Early Childhood Learning Center that's got everyone worked up. There's been a lot of frustration about cost, transparency, and process around the ECLC project. Meanwhile, the Georgia Senate passed a bill requiring voter approval for CSD bonds over $20 million.
stories.body:
The drama around Decatur's proposed Early Childhood Learning Center has reached the point where the school board is hosting a special community meeting to try to calm things down. Tuesday's session is supposed to address questions about the upcoming budget, the ECLC project, and the Board Management Oversight Plan, but really it's about damage control after months of heated community meetings.

The frustration isn't just about the money, though that's certainly part of it. Parents and residents are upset about what they see as a lack of transparency in how the district has handled the ECLC planning process. Cost estimates keep changing, the need for the facility is being questioned, and people feel like they're being asked to write a blank check without getting straight answers about what they're actually buying.

To make matters more complicated, the Georgia Senate just passed a bill requiring voter approval for any City Schools of Decatur bonds over $20 million. That means if the ECLC moves forward at its current projected cost, residents would get to vote on it whether the school board likes it or not. The bill now heads to the House, and you can bet Decatur voters are watching closely to see if they'll actually get a say in this expensive project.

stories.headline: Park Pride Tackles Climate Reality at Annual Conference
stories.summary: Southeast's largest parks and greenspace conference brought hundreds to the Botanical Gardens to figure out how parks can help us survive climate change. Park Pride's 25th annual event focused on building resilient parks and communities as a key climate strategy. The reality check: 'Maybe no city is a climate refuge' anymore, so we better start planning for that.
stories.body:
The annual Parks & Greenspace Conference used to be about maintaining what we had, but this year's theme made it clear that era is over. 'Resilient Parks, Resilient Communities' drew hundreds of attendees from across the Southeast to the Atlanta Botanical Gardens to grapple with a fundamental question: how do you build parks that can handle whatever climate change throws at us?

Park Pride Education Director Eli Dickerson opened with a sobering reality check: 'Maybe no city is a climate refuge.' That's a big shift from the conventional wisdom that places like Atlanta might be insulated from the worst climate impacts. If nowhere is safe, then every community needs to start thinking about how green spaces can help people survive extreme heat, flooding, and other climate disasters.

The conference workshops focused on practical solutions like urban heat island reduction, stormwater management through parks, and designing green spaces that can handle both drought and flooding. It's the kind of unglamorous but essential work that cities need to be doing now, not later. Parks aren't just nice amenities anymore, they're critical infrastructure for community survival in a changing climate. The fact that this conversation is happening at the region's biggest parks conference shows how seriously people are taking the challenge.
stories.imageAlt: Attendees at Parks & Greenspace Conference discussing climate resilience

stories.headline: DeKalb Parents Take School Districting Fight to the Streets
stories.summary: Parents and kids from Oak Grove and Evansdale elementary schools protested outside the Lakeside High meeting where DeKalb County was holding another Student Assignment Project session. They're demanding a student-first approach instead of what they're calling a 'farce' of a public input process. Signs called for small schools and people-first policies as the district considers major boundary changes.
stories.body:
The frustration with DeKalb County's Student Assignment Project boiled over into the streets Sunday, with parents and children from Oak Grove and Evansdale elementary schools protesting outside Lakeside High School. They carried signs advocating for small schools and a people-first approach to school districting, making it clear they don't trust the process the district is running.

Samuel Howe, an Oak Grove parent, summed up the sentiment pretty bluntly: 'This is not a process. This is a farce, and they're not listening.' That's the level of distrust the district is dealing with as it tries to redraw school boundaries and potentially close schools. Parents feel like they're being given lip service while decisions have already been made behind closed doors.

The Student Assignment Project is supposed to address enrollment imbalances and facility issues across the district, but the community input sessions have become contentious affairs where parents feel unheard. The fact that families brought their kids to protest shows how personal this issue has become. When parents are willing to take their children to demonstrations about school policy, that's a sign the district has lost the community's confidence in the process.
stories.imageAlt: Parents and children holding protest signs outside Lakeside High School

stories.headline: 8-Foot Python Shows Up on the BeltLine in Broad Daylight
stories.summary: Someone spotted an 8-foot python along the Atlanta BeltLine and captured video of a man handling the snake like it was no big deal. Environmental officials are warning people not to touch wildlife they find, especially massive snakes that definitely don't belong in Georgia. The snake owner is unknown, and reptile experts are concerned about people handling dangerous animals without proper training.
stories.body:
The Atlanta BeltLine has seen some wild stuff over the years, but an 8-foot python slithering along the trail in broad daylight might be a new one. Joseph Ragland was walking the BeltLine when he spotted the massive snake and started recording, capturing the moment when 'somebody's uncle comes out of nowhere and starts yanking it out of the bush' like he was picking up a garden hose.

Ragland described the surreal scene: 'All of the sudden there's 10 feet of snake laying on the beltline.' The video shows a man confidently handling what appears to be a python, though nobody knows if he was the owner or just a very bold Good Samaritan. Environmental officials are not amused, warning people to stay away from wildlife they encounter, especially exotic species that could be dangerous.

Jason Clark from Southeastern Reptile Rescue called the video 'very concerning,' pointing out that handling large constrictors without proper training is asking for trouble. Pythons that size can seriously injure or kill someone if they wrap around a person's neck or torso. The bigger question is how an 8-foot python ended up on the BeltLine in the first place, and whether Atlanta has a bigger exotic pet problem than anyone realized.
stories.imageAlt: Large python being handled by man along the Atlanta BeltLine

stories.headline: National Archives Brings Founding Documents to Atlanta
stories.summary: While one side of Hartsfield-Jackson was dealing with airport chaos, the other side welcomed the Freedom Plane carrying historic founding documents for America's 250th anniversary tour. An 1823 Declaration of Independence engraving and Senate markup of the Bill of Rights will be on display at the Atlanta History Center from March 27 to April 12. Free admission but you need to register online.
stories.body:
Sometimes timing is everything. While thousands of travelers were stuck in TSA lines on one side of Hartsfield-Jackson, the Freedom Plane was quietly landing on the other side with some of America's most precious founding documents. The National Archives' eight-city tour commemorating the country's 250th anniversary brought historic items like an 1823 engraving of the Declaration of Independence and a Senate markup of the Bill of Rights.

The documents will be on display at the Atlanta History Center from March 27 through April 12, and admission is free if you pre-register online. The center has already signed up 12,000 visitors, which shows there's real appetite for this kind of historical programming. It's also smart timing with all the national conversations about democracy and founding principles happening right now.

The contrast between the airport chaos and this quiet arrival of founding documents feels pretty symbolic of where we are as a country. On one side, basic government functions are breaking down due to political dysfunction. On the other side, we're celebrating the documents that created the system in the first place. The History Center recommends weekday visits for the best experience, which probably applies to both seeing historic documents and flying through Atlanta these days.
stories.imageAlt: Historic documents being unloaded from the Freedom Plane at Atlanta airport
